summary: #recompute re-runs A* routing (plus two full-city snapToStreet scans each) for every consecutive waypoint pair on every single edit, and loaded saved routes get a waypoint per street, making each click O(routeStreets x cityStreets).
failure_scenario: Loading a saved route creates R+1 waypoints via #waypointsFromStreets, so on an edit of a 200-street route every click/undo/reverse runs ~200 route() calls = ~400 snapToStreet scans over the whole city network plus 200 A* searches — seconds of main-thread jank per edit in a large city. #addWaypoint additionally routes the new segment once for the reachability check, then #recompute discards that result and routes it again. Cheaper: cache the street list per unchanged waypoint pair and only route the appended/changed segment, and resolve stored waypoints via RouteGraph's O(1) quantized-cell node lookup instead of the O(all-streets) snapToStreet scan.

summary: snapToStreet iterates every street feature in the city — even when a regionId filter is passed — doing at least one haversine per feature, and it runs once per animation frame on mousemove and twice per route() call.
failure_scenario: Once a neighborhood is selected, #updateGhost calls snapToStreet(point, regionId) on every pointer frame (~60x/s); each call scans all tens of thousands of city streets, ~200-400 ms CPU per second of mouse movement, when only a few hundred region streets are candidates. Cheaper: bucket #features by region_id at construction, or index street endpoints in the existing quantized-cell grid and search outward from the pointer's cell.

summary: Inside #recompute's per-street loop (and again in #drawStreetList at line 1242), each routed street is located with a linear this.#streetData.features.find() over the entire city street array — O(routeStreets x cityStreets) per edit/load.
failure_scenario: Every recompute and every saved-route load does a full linear scan of the city's street array per street in the route: a 200-street route in a 30k-street city is ~6M comparisons per edit. Cheaper: RouteGraph already holds a Map<street_edge_id, feature>; expose a getFeature(streetId) accessor and use it in both places.

summary: getRoutesForUser recomputes ST_Transform(26918)+ST_Length over every street of every route on each request, and getStreetGeometries pulls all routes' full street geometries so RouteService can re-encode thumbnail polylines — values that only change when a route's street list changes.
failure_scenario: Runs on every dashboard load, every RouteBuilder page load (SavedRoutesPanel.refresh), and after each save/update; for a user with dozens of routes spanning hundreds of streets, every hit re-projects and re-measures thousands of geometries in Postgres and re-encodes thousands of LineStrings in Scala, producing identical output each time. Cheaper: persist distance_meters and encoded_polyline (and street_count) as columns on route, written once when the street list changes.

summary: The route progress bar recomputes taskContainer.totalLineDistanceInNeighborhood — turf.length over every task in the route — on every mission-progress update, though the route's total distance is constant for the session.
failure_scenario: updateMissionProgress fires on each pano position change while walking a route; each call maps turf.length over all route tasks, recomputing the same constant total every step. Cheaper: compute once when tasks finish loading and cache it.

summary: getRoutesForUser runs the usage-counts and street-geometries queries sequentially in a for-comprehension though they are independent, serializing two DB round trips on every route-listing request.
failure_scenario: Each listing waits for getUsageCounts before issuing getStreetGeometries (the slower query), adding one full query's latency per request for no ordering reason. Cheaper: start both db.run futures before the for-comprehension or zip them.

summary: #updateStats re-measures every street in the route with turf.length on each recompute even though RouteGraph computed and cached each street's length in #featureLengths at construction.
failure_scenario: Every click/undo/reverse re-runs turf.length over all route streets to produce lengths RouteGraph already holds; #handleRouteSaved repeats the same reduce a third time for guest records. Cheaper: expose RouteGraph's cached per-street lengths or accumulate from the weightM values already summed during routing.
